title: JTBD Analysis: General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ | Output Types | Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 622110 | Diagnose, treat, and stabilize patients presenting with acute medical and surgical conditions | Emergency/Trauma Care · Elective Inpatient Surgery · Medical Inpatient · ICU · Outpatient/Day Procedure | L7 (Ecosystem) |

### 1.2 Segments (by circumstance)

| # | Segment | Circumstance | Alternative divergence test |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Psychiatric / behavioral health inpatient | Patient on involuntary hold, suicide watch, or acute behavioral episode; exit = life-safety failure | RTLS wander tags vs. door alarm guard vs. wired ACS are non-interchangeable |
| S2 | Cognitively impaired inpatient | Dementia, delirium, TBI — wanders without understanding consequences | Physical door barrier vs. wearable geofence serve different needs |
| S3 | Pediatric / NICU patient | Infant or minor requiring protection against unauthorized removal | Infant RF protection systems vs. exit alarm guards are distinct categories |
| S4 | Controlled-access zone occupant | Staff moving through OR, pharmacy, blood bank, sterile supply — identity verification required | Full ACS (credential + lock) vs. exit alarm-only are distinct jobs |
| S5 | General inpatient | Medical-surgical ward — compliance-driven, not clinical containment | Basic mechanical alarm vs. IoT-monitored exit differ on documentation value |
| S6 | Facility & building safety manager | Manages exit compliance, JCI audits, maintenance across multi-wing campus | Manual patrol vs. centralized IoT dashboard are operationally distinct |

### 1.3 Process VN (from VN file)

Core L6 (sequential): L6a Patient Access → L6b Diagnostics → L6c Surgical Intervention → L6d Inpatient Care → L6e Pharmacy → L6f Discharge

Horizontal L6: **L6h1 Facility & Building Safety** ← product entry point | L6h2 Infection Prevention | L6h3 Clinical Information Systems

> **Hospital-specific note:** NFPA 101 §18 mandates **defend-in-place** — patients are relocated horizontally to adjacent smoke compartments, not evacuated. Emergency exits serve two primary functions: (1) unauthorized elopement prevention, (2) smoke compartment boundary control. Full building evacuation is last resort. [WEB — NFPA 101 Life Safety Code, Coffman Engineers 2024 analysis]

### 1.4 Product BOM
Service NAICS — BOM not applicable. L6h1 (Facility & Building Safety) runs as a horizontal support function across all core L6 steps.

### 1.5 Edges (VN ↔ Output Types)
L6h1 activates for **all 5 output types** — building safety infrastructure is universal. The Emergency Exit Security Device (L5 under L6h1) has no output-type exemption; every patient pathway requires compliant exits.

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor unit** | Emergency Exit Security Device |
| **Anchor level** | L5 (standalone system) |
| **Route** | PROCESS_VN — product is used in hospital operations, never part of patient output |
| **Parent unit** | Facility & Building Safety (L6h1) |
| **Parent job family** | Maintain building infrastructure, fire safety, emergency exit security, and physical access control across all clinical and support areas |

Product does NOT anchor at L4 (it is not a sub-component of another system being sold here; it is the complete system purchased by the hospital). Anchor level L5 is correct.

### 2.2 Lens

| Route | Function verb | Beneficiary | Lens statement |
|---|---|---|---|
| PROCESS_VN, L5 under L6h1 | Maintain (from L6h1 job_family) | Facility & Building Safety function | "What emergency exit security and remote monitoring capabilities does Facility & Building Safety need to maintain compliant exit management across all clinical zones?" |

Active segments for this lens: S1, S2, S3, S5, S6. S4 (controlled-access zones) is secondary — its primary alternatives are full ACS systems at a different L5 node.

### 2.3 Product Job Statement

> **"Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus"**

Abstraction test: 3+ alternative categories qualify (wired exit bars, RTLS wander management, mechanical alarm-only, manual patrol). Passes.

### 2.4 Alternatives (5 per Core Job)

| Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent trade-offs |
|---|---|---|
| **Wired alarmed exit bars** (Von Duprin, Corbin Russwin, Sargent) | 46171600 | Requires conduit and cable at every door; licensed electrician; disruptive retrofit in occupied wards; no wireless campus dashboard |
| **Mechanical alarm guards, no IoT** (GfS Basic, ABUS) | 46171600 | Zero remote monitoring; alarm event discoverable only on-site; no automated compliance documentation; maintenance requires physical inspection rounds |
| **Wireless electronic exit control** (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic) | 46171600 | Higher unit cost; typically BACnet-integrated; more complex configuration and commissioning; heavier BMS dependency |
| **RTLS wander management** (Securitas Healthcare, CenTrak, RF Technologies) | 46191700 | Patient-worn tag required; no physical door barrier; covers elopement but does not satisfy exit compliance documentation; ~3–5× higher system cost [WEB] |
| **Manual patrol + paper inspection logs** [Status Quo] | — | Zero device cost; 100% labor-dependent; no real-time detection; patrol gaps expose liability; documented compliance gaps risk JCI non-conformance findings |

> **Existential flag:** RTLS wander management eliminates the door-alarm category for psychiatric/dementia elopement use cases (S1, S2) if patient tagging becomes standard of care. Monitor.

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Output types activated | L4 nodes in play |
|---|---|---|
| Psychiatric ward elopement prevention (S1) | Medical Inpatient, ICU | Guard/Handle Latch Mechanism, Wireless State Transmitter |
| Dementia / delirium wander prevention (S2) | Medical Inpatient | Guard/Handle Latch Mechanism, Alarm Horn Assembly |
| NICU / pediatric unauthorized removal (S3) | Medical Inpatient | Alarm Horn Assembly, Guard Mechanism |
| Pharmacy / blood bank perimeter control (S4) | All inpatient | Guard Mechanism, Key Override, Wireless Transmitter |
| Fire safety compliance documentation (S5, S6) | All output types | Wireless State Transmitter, Battery Power Supply, BMS gateway |
| Smoke compartment boundary monitoring | Emergency/Trauma, ICU | Wireless State Transmitter, Alarm Horn Assembly |

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Stakeholder Roles in Scope

| Role | P-levels | Person |
|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | P1 (all), P2, P3, P4, P5 | Facilities / engineering technician operating and maintaining devices |
| **Job Overseer** | P1 (own), P2, P4 | Facilities Director, Safety Officer, Chief Compliance Officer |
| **Job Beneficiary** | P2, P5 | Hospital patients (psychiatric, dementia, pediatric) — protected from harm |
| **Lifecycle Support** | P1 (own) | Security integrator (install), facilities tech (maintain), fire safety inspector (validate) |
| **Purchase Executor** | P1 (Acq) | VP Facilities, CFO |
| **Purchase Influencer** | P2, P4 | Joint Commission surveyor, risk manager, infection preventionist |

### 3.2 P1 Lifecycle Stages by Alternative

| Stage | Wired exit bars | Mechanical alarm (no IoT) | IoT alarm guard (Türwächter) | RTLS wander mgmt |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquire** | Spec per door type; procure cable; engage electrician; building permit in occupied wings | Spec EN 179/1125 variant; order hardware; minor scope | Spec variant + receiver/gateway; plan wireless topology; configure network | Procure tags, readers, server; design patient enrollment workflow |
| **Prepare** | Cut conduit, pull cable, terminate wiring, test circuits — 2–4 hrs/door | Surface-mount on handle; test swing; 15–20 min/door | Surface-mount; pair to receiver; configure gateway; test wireless range; commission dashboard; ~25–35 min/door | Install zone readers; enroll patients and staff; integrate with nurse call |
| **Use** | Alarm on-site; dispatch staff from field | Alarm on-site; local response only | Alarm event → dashboard alert → response dispatched with exact door location | Tag at zone boundary → nurse-station alert |
| **Maintain** | Annual inspection; cable fault diagnosis; re-termination | Physical battery check and alarm test each door | Remote battery monitoring; wireless coverage audit; firmware updates | Tag battery replacement; reader calibration; server maintenance |
| **Dispose** | Cable removal (structural works); hardware deinstall | Device swap; no cabling | Device swap; reconfigure receiver/gateway mapping | Tag collection; server decommission; patient data purge |

**Key insight:** Wired bars have highest P1 burden (Prepare + Maintain). Mechanical-only eliminates Prepare/Maintain overhead but creates a Usage gap (no real-time detection). IoT alarm guard creates the lowest operational burden relative to monitoring capability.

### 3.3 Job Map — Relevance Filter

| # | Step | Relevant? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | YES | Specifying compliance requirements, door count, zone classifications, wireless topology per NFPA 101/JCI standards |
| 2 | LOCATE | YES | Mapping 50–500+ exit doors across wards, wings, and floors — often incomplete in legacy buildings |
| 3 | PREPARE | YES | Retrofit installation in occupied clinical zones without disrupting patient care |
| 4 | CONFIRM | YES | Testing alarm function, wireless coverage gaps, documentation baseline before go-live |
| 5 | EXECUTE | YES | Core function: securing exits, triggering alarms, processing key overrides |
| 6 | MONITOR | YES | Centralized real-time status visibility across campus — the primary IoT value |
| 7 | MODIFY | YES | Responding to alarm events, ward reconfigurations, battery replacements |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | YES | JCI survey documentation, compliance log generation, preventive maintenance scheduling |

All 8 steps relevant — L6h1 is a continuous-operation compliance function.

### 3.4 Error Statements (P1 + P2 Needs) — 18 Statements

| # | Statement | Step | Error type | Imp | Sat | OS | Impact | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | Minimize the likelihood of incorrect product variant specification (EN 179 vs. EN 1125) causing ≥1 non-conforming door installation identified at first JCI fire-safety survey | DEFINE | ACCURACY | 8 | 5 | 11 | safety | false |
| N02 | Increase the ability to document a complete hospital door inventory (type, standard, zone class) across a 300-bed campus within 2 working days | DEFINE | EFFORT | 7 | 4 | 10 | time | false |
| N03 | Minimize variability in wireless signal strength across reinforced-concrete hospital floors that causes >5% of installed devices to fall below reliable reporting threshold | LOCATE | CONSISTENCY | 8 | 4 | 12 | reliability | **true** |
| N04 | Minimize installation time per door in occupied inpatient wards to <30 minutes without conduit, cable, or licensed electrician | PREPARE | EFFORT | 8 | 5 | 11 | cost | false |
| N05 | Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware configurations (glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, legacy panic bars) in pre-1990 hospital wings | PREPARE | ROBUSTNESS | 7 | 5 | 9 | cost | **true** |
| N06 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation requiring costly repeater additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting coverage | CONFIRM | DETECTION | 8 | 4 | 12 | cost | **true** |
| N07 | Increase the ability to verify single-motion door release compliance per EN 179 / EN 1125 for 100 installed devices within a 5-working-day JCI preparation window | CONFIRM | THROUGHPUT | 8 | 5 | 11 | time | false |
| N08 | Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized emergency exit activation going undetected for >5 minutes during overnight or weekend reduced-staffing periods | EXECUTE | DETECTION | 10 | 2 | 18 | safety | false |
| N09 | Minimize the frequency of false alarms on high-traffic emergency exits exceeding 2 events per week due to incidental door contact by clinical staff | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | 8 | 4 | 12 | time | false |
| N10 | Increase the ability to provide authorized clinical staff with immediate key-bypass access on emergency exits during acute clinical emergencies in <10 seconds without triggering a facility-wide alarm response | EXECUTE | EFFORT | 8 | 6 | 10 | time | false |
| N11 | Increase the ability to monitor battery status for all exit devices across a 200+ door hospital campus from a single dashboard with >95% real-time accuracy | MONITOR | DETECTION | 9 | 2 | 16 | reliability | false |
| N12 | Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures exceeding 1% due to RF interference from hospital imaging equipment (MRI, CT) in adjacent zones | MONITOR | ROBUSTNESS | 9 | 4 | 14 | reliability | **true** |
| N13 | Increase the ability to correlate emergency exit alarm events with concurrent patient elopement incidents in the incident reporting system with <30 seconds of timestamp resolution | MONITOR | ACCURACY | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | false |
| N14 | Minimize the time required to reconfigure exit device zone assignments when hospital wards are repurposed or patient population mix changes, to <2 hours per ward | MODIFY | EFFORT | 7 | 4 | 10 | time | false |
| N15 | Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations in adjacent rooms | MODIFY | EFFORT | 7 | 5 | 9 | cost | **true** |
| N16 | Increase the ability to generate Joint Commission-compliant Environment of Care documentation for all emergency exit devices covering 12 months of operational logs without manual data aggregation | CONCLUDE | THROUGHPUT | 9 | 2 | 16 | cost | false |
| N17 | Minimize the likelihood of GDPR-regulated door event logs containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established processing basis under EU 2016/679 | CONCLUDE | ACCURACY | 8 | 4 | 12 | safety | **true** |
| N18 | Minimize variability in alarm response time across door types (lever-handle EN 179 vs. panic-bar EN 1125 configurations) to within ±0.2 seconds of each other under equivalent activation force | EXECUTE | CONSISTENCY | 7 | 6 | 8 | safety | false |

### 3.5 P3–P5 Bridge Needs — 3 Statements

| # | Level | Retrieval | Bridge | Statement | Imp | Sat | OS | product_related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N19 | **P3 Role** | "By managing exit compliance systematically, the Safety Officer becomes the person whose department never fails a JCI survey" | Product must deliver automated compliance evidence so they can demonstrate mastery without manual effort | Increase the ability to demonstrate real-time emergency exit compliance status to hospital leadership and external surveyors on demand, without manual inspection rounds [KPI: on-demand compliance availability rate] | 8 | 3 | 13 | false |
| N20 | **P4 Image** | "Watchers: JCI surveyor, CMS inspector, hospital CEO. They must think: this facility runs a proactive, data-driven safety program" | Product must show audit trail and proactive alerting that is visible at survey time | Minimize the likelihood of visible emergency exit compliance gaps during an unannounced Joint Commission Environment of Care survey [KPI: non-conformance findings per survey cycle] | 9 | 4 | 14 | false |
| N21 | **P5 Emotional** [hypothesis] | "On-duty security staff want to feel: immediately informed and in control. They dread: finding out about a patient elopement hours after it occurred" | Product must push real-time location-specific alerts to security staff with no delay | Minimize the delay between an emergency exit activation event and receipt of an actionable location-specific alert on the security officer's device to <15 seconds [KPI: alert-to-dispatch time] | 7 | 2 | 12 | false |

### 3.6 ODI Opportunity Summary (top 5 underserved needs)

| # | Statement (short) | OS | Importance | Satisfaction | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N08 | Undetected unauthorized activation >5 min, overnight | **18** | 10 | 2 | **Underserved** |
| N11 | Battery status monitoring, 200+ door dashboard | **16** | 9 | 2 | **Underserved** |
| N16 | JCI-compliant 12-month log generation, auto | **16** | 9 | 2 | **Underserved** |
| N12 | RF interference failure from MRI/CT zones | **14** | 9 | 4 | **Underserved** |
| N20 | JCI survey compliance gap visibility | **14** | 9 | 4 | **Underserved** |

### 3.7 Yield Check

| Metric | Target | Actual | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Error statements total | 15–30 | 21 | ✓ |
| Needs per relevant job step (≥2) | ≥2 each | 2–3 each | ✓ |
| Market-inherent (product_related=false) | 60–70% | 14/21 = 67% | ✓ |
| Technology-specific (product_related=true) | 30–40% | 7/21 = 33% | ✓ |
| "Minimize" ratio | ~60% | 13/21 = 62% | ✓ |
| "Increase" ratio | ~40% | 8/21 = 38% | ✓ |
| Job map steps covered | 8 | 8 | ✓ |

### 3.8 Constraint Interaction Notes

| Constraint | Needs directly affected |
|---|---|
| C4 (IoT battery life) | N11, N15 |
| C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in) | N03, N12 — integration friction with hospital BACnet BMS |
| C8 (GDPR / HIPAA) | N17 — door event logs require DPA + DPIA in EU hospitals |
| C10 (EN 13637 in access-controlled wards) | N01, N06 — combined ACS + exit control configurations |

---

sections.title: Validation (QA Self-Check)
sections.content:
| # | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| V1 | Every statement starts with "Minimize..." or "Increase the ability to..." with exactly one KPI | PASS |
| V2 | product_related=true only if need disappears with wired/non-IoT alternative | PASS — N03, N05, N06, N12, N15, N17 all technology-caused |
| V3 | No unrealistic importance/satisfaction patterns; N08 (safety/elopement) = 10 — appropriate | PASS |
| V4 | Impact category matches KPI | PASS |
| V5 | ≥2 needs per step; 67% market-inherent, 33% tech-specific | PASS |
| V6 | Zero reference to "GfS", "Türwächter", "EasyWave", "868 MHz", "piezo" in need statements | PASS |
| V7 | Anchor at L5 — standalone system level correct for a complete exit security unit | PASS |
| V8 | Product job statement includes parent VN unit ("Facility & Building Safety") and context qualifier | PASS |

---

sections.content:
### JobMap

```json
{
  "naics_code": "622110",
  "market_name": "General Medical and Surgical Hospitals",
  "anchor_vn_unit": "Emergency Exit Security Device",
  "anchor_level": "L5",
  "parent_vn_unit": "Facility & Building Safety",
  "parent_level": "L6",
  "product_job_statement": "Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus",
  "cfj_statement": "Diagnose, treat, and stabilize patients presenting with acute medical and surgical conditions — from initial triage through inpatient care to discharge or transfer to post-acute services",
  "offering_level": "L7",
  "route": "PROCESS_VN",
  "hospital_specific_note": "NFPA 101 §18 defend-in-place strategy: emergency exits serve elopement prevention and smoke compartment boundary control — not primary evacuation"
}
```

### JobStep[]

```json
[
  { "step_number": 1, "step_name": "DEFINE", "statement": "Specify compliance requirements, door count, zone classifications, and wireless topology per NFPA 101 / JCI / EN 179/1125 standards", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Variant selection (EN 179 vs. EN 1125, IoT vs. basic) is determined at spec stage" },
  { "step_number": 2, "step_name": "LOCATE", "statement": "Map all fire-code-designated emergency exit doors across wards, wings, and floors — often 50–500+ doors in legacy multi-wing hospitals", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Wireless topology design requires door inventory and RF coverage assessment" },
  { "step_number": 3, "step_name": "PREPARE", "statement": "Install devices on door hardware and commission wireless receiver/gateway network without disrupting occupied clinical zones", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Retrofit installation without conduit is the product's key advantage; installation quality determines coverage" },
  { "step_number": 4, "step_name": "CONFIRM", "statement": "Verify alarm function, wireless coverage, key-override operation, and establish compliance documentation baseline before go-live", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Coverage gaps and compliance baseline must be confirmed before clinical handover" },
  { "step_number": 5, "step_name": "EXECUTE", "statement": "Operate continuously: secure exits, trigger alarms on unauthorized activation, process authorized key overrides, maintain fail-safe mechanical alarm independence", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Core operating function; elopement detection and false alarm rate are primary performance KPIs" },
  { "step_number": 6, "step_name": "MONITOR", "statement": "Centrally observe real-time door status, alarm events, and battery levels across campus from dashboard; detect anomalies before they become incidents", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "IoT monitoring is the primary value differentiator over mechanical-only alternatives" },
  { "step_number": 7, "step_name": "MODIFY", "statement": "Respond to alarm events with location-specific dispatch; reconfigure devices when ward functions change; replace batteries on maintenance schedule", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Ward reorganizations and battery management are ongoing operational burdens in hospital settings" },
  { "step_number": 8, "step_name": "CONCLUDE", "statement": "Generate JCI Environment of Care documentation, validate 12-month operational logs, schedule preventive maintenance cycles, and close compliance audit cycles", "relevant_to_product": true, "relevance_rationale": "Automated compliance documentation is the core ROI justification for IoT variant over manual inspection" }
]
```

rows.CFJ: Diagnose, treat, and stabilize patients presenting with acute medical and surgical conditions
rows.Output Types: Emergency/Trauma Care · Elective Inpatient Surgery · Medical Inpatient · ICU · Outpatient/Day Procedure

rows.Circumstance: Patient on involuntary hold, suicide watch, or acute behavioral episode; exit = life-safety failure
rows.Alternative divergence test: RTLS wander tags vs. door alarm guard vs. wired ACS are non-interchangeable

rows.Circumstance: Dementia, delirium, TBI — wanders without understanding consequences
rows.Alternative divergence test: Physical door barrier vs. wearable geofence serve different needs

rows.Circumstance: Infant or minor requiring protection against unauthorized removal
rows.Alternative divergence test: Infant RF protection systems vs. exit alarm guards are distinct categories

rows.Circumstance: Staff moving through OR, pharmacy, blood bank, sterile supply — identity verification required
rows.Alternative divergence test: Full ACS (credential + lock) vs. exit alarm-only are distinct jobs

rows.Circumstance: Medical-surgical ward — compliance-driven, not clinical containment
rows.Alternative divergence test: Basic mechanical alarm vs. IoT-monitored exit differ on documentation value

rows.Circumstance: Manages exit compliance, JCI audits, maintenance across multi-wing campus
rows.Alternative divergence test: Manual patrol vs. centralized IoT dashboard are operationally distinct

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — product is used in hospital operations, never part of patient output
Maintain building infrastructure, fire safety, emergency exit security, and physical access control across all clinical and support areas

rows.Lens statement: "What emergency exit security and remote monitoring capabilities does Facility & Building Safety need to maintain compliant exit management across all clinical zones?"

rows.Alternative: **Wired alarmed exit bars** (Von Duprin, Corbin Russwin, Sargent)
rows.Inherent trade-offs: Requires conduit and cable at every door; licensed electrician; disruptive retrofit in occupied wards; no wireless campus dashboard

rows.Alternative: **Mechanical alarm guards, no IoT** (GfS Basic, ABUS)
rows.Inherent trade-offs: Zero remote monitoring; alarm event discoverable only on-site; no automated compliance documentation; maintenance requires physical inspection rounds

rows.Alternative: **Wireless electronic exit control** (dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic)
rows.Inherent trade-offs: Higher unit cost; typically BACnet-integrated; more complex configuration and commissioning; heavier BMS dependency

rows.Alternative: **RTLS wander management** (Securitas Healthcare, CenTrak, RF Technologies)
rows.Inherent trade-offs: Patient-worn tag required; no physical door barrier; covers elopement but does not satisfy exit compliance documentation; ~3–5× higher system cost [WEB]
Zero device cost; 100% labor-dependent; no real-time detection; patrol gaps expose liability; documented compliance gaps risk JCI non-conformance findings

rows.L4 nodes in play: Guard/Handle Latch Mechanism, Wireless State Transmitter
Guard/Handle Latch Mechanism, Alarm Horn Assembly
Guard Mechanism, Key Override, Wireless Transmitter
Wireless State Transmitter, Battery Power Supply, BMS gateway
Wireless State Transmitter, Alarm Horn Assembly

rows.Person: Facilities / engineering technician operating and maintaining devices
Facilities Director, Safety Officer, Chief Compliance Officer
Hospital patients (psychiatric, dementia, pediatric) — protected from harm
Security integrator (install), facilities tech (maintain), fire safety inspector (validate)
Joint Commission surveyor, risk manager, infection preventionist

rows.Wired exit bars: Spec per door type; procure cable; engage electrician; building permit in occupied wings
rows.Mechanical alarm (no IoT): Spec EN 179/1125 variant; order hardware; minor scope
rows.IoT alarm guard (Türwächter): Spec variant + receiver/gateway; plan wireless topology; configure network
rows.RTLS wander mgmt: Procure tags, readers, server; design patient enrollment workflow

rows.Wired exit bars: Cut conduit, pull cable, terminate wiring, test circuits — 2–4 hrs/door
rows.Mechanical alarm (no IoT): Surface-mount on handle; test swing; 15–20 min/door
rows.IoT alarm guard (Türwächter): Surface-mount; pair to receiver; configure gateway; test wireless range; commission dashboard; ~25–35 min/door
rows.RTLS wander mgmt: Install zone readers; enroll patients and staff; integrate with nurse call

rows.Wired exit bars: Alarm on-site; dispatch staff from field
rows.IoT alarm guard (Türwächter): Alarm event → dashboard alert → response dispatched with exact door location
rows.RTLS wander mgmt: Tag at zone boundary → nurse-station alert

rows.Wired exit bars: Annual inspection; cable fault diagnosis; re-termination
rows.Mechanical alarm (no IoT): Physical battery check and alarm test each door
rows.IoT alarm guard (Türwächter): Remote battery monitoring; wireless coverage audit; firmware updates
rows.RTLS wander mgmt: Tag battery replacement; reader calibration; server maintenance
Tag collection; server decommission; patient data purge

rows.Rationale: Specifying compliance requirements, door count, zone classifications, wireless topology per NFPA 101/JCI standards
Mapping 50–500+ exit doors across wards, wings, and floors — often incomplete in legacy buildings
Retrofit installation in occupied clinical zones without disrupting patient care
Testing alarm function, wireless coverage gaps, documentation baseline before go-live
Core function: securing exits, triggering alarms, processing key overrides
Centralized real-time status visibility across campus — the primary IoT value
Responding to alarm events, ward reconfigurations, battery replacements
JCI survey documentation, compliance log generation, preventive maintenance scheduling

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect product variant specification (EN 179 vs. EN 1125) causing ≥1 non-conforming door installation identified at first JCI fire-safety survey
Increase the ability to document a complete hospital door inventory (type, standard, zone class) across a 300-bed campus within 2 working days
Minimize variability in wireless signal strength across reinforced-concrete hospital floors that causes >5% of installed devices to fall below reliable reporting threshold
Minimize installation time per door in occupied inpatient wards to <30 minutes without conduit, cable, or licensed electrician
Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware configurations (glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, legacy panic bars) in pre-1990 hospital wings
Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation requiring costly repeater additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting coverage
Increase the ability to verify single-motion door release compliance per EN 179 / EN 1125 for 100 installed devices within a 5-working-day JCI preparation window
Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized emergency exit activation going undetected for >5 minutes during overnight or weekend reduced-staffing periods
Minimize the frequency of false alarms on high-traffic emergency exits exceeding 2 events per week due to incidental door contact by clinical staff

Increase the ability to provide authorized clinical staff with immediate key-bypass access on emergency exits during acute clinical emergencies in <10 seconds without triggering a facility-wide alarm response
Increase the ability to monitor battery status for all exit devices across a 200+ door hospital campus from a single dashboard with >95% real-time accuracy
Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures exceeding 1% due to RF interference from hospital imaging equipment (MRI, CT) in adjacent zones
Increase the ability to correlate emergency exit alarm events with concurrent patient elopement incidents in the incident reporting system with <30 seconds of timestamp resolution
Minimize the time required to reconfigure exit device zone assignments when hospital wards are repurposed or patient population mix changes, to <2 hours per ward
Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations in adjacent rooms
Increase the ability to generate Joint Commission-compliant Environment of Care documentation for all emergency exit devices covering 12 months of operational logs without manual data aggregation
Minimize the likelihood of GDPR-regulated door event logs containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established processing basis under EU 2016/679
Minimize variability in alarm response time across door types (lever-handle EN 179 vs. panic-bar EN 1125 configurations) to within ±0.2 seconds of each other under equivalent activation force

entities.product_job_statement: Help Facility & Building Safety to maintain authorized access boundaries at emergency exits while guaranteeing single-motion escape compliance across all clinical zones, and provide real-time alerts on unauthorized activation events across the campus
entities.cfj_statement: Diagnose, treat, and stabilize patients presenting with acute medical and surgical conditions — from initial triage through inpatient care to discharge or transfer to post-acute services
entities.hospital_specific_note: NFPA 101 §18 defend-in-place strategy: emergency exits serve elopement prevention and smoke compartment boundary control — not primary evacuation

entities.statement: Specify compliance requirements, door count, zone classifications, and wireless topology per NFPA 101 / JCI / EN 179/1125 standards
entities.relevance_rationale: Variant selection (EN 179 vs. EN 1125, IoT vs. basic) is determined at spec stage

entities.statement: Map all fire-code-designated emergency exit doors across wards, wings, and floors — often 50–500+ doors in legacy multi-wing hospitals
entities.relevance_rationale: Wireless topology design requires door inventory and RF coverage assessment

entities.statement: Install devices on door hardware and commission wireless receiver/gateway network without disrupting occupied clinical zones
entities.relevance_rationale: Retrofit installation without conduit is the product's key advantage; installation quality determines coverage

entities.statement: Verify alarm function, wireless coverage, key-override operation, and establish compliance documentation baseline before go-live
entities.relevance_rationale: Coverage gaps and compliance baseline must be confirmed before clinical handover

entities.statement: Operate continuously: secure exits, trigger alarms on unauthorized activation, process authorized key overrides, maintain fail-safe mechanical alarm independence
entities.relevance_rationale: Core operating function; elopement detection and false alarm rate are primary performance KPIs

entities.statement: Centrally observe real-time door status, alarm events, and battery levels across campus from dashboard; detect anomalies before they become incidents
entities.relevance_rationale: IoT monitoring is the primary value differentiator over mechanical-only alternatives

entities.statement: Respond to alarm events with location-specific dispatch; reconfigure devices when ward functions change; replace batteries on maintenance schedule
entities.relevance_rationale: Ward reorganizations and battery management are ongoing operational burdens in hospital settings

entities.statement: Generate JCI Environment of Care documentation, validate 12-month operational logs, schedule preventive maintenance cycles, and close compliance audit cycles
entities.relevance_rationale: Automated compliance documentation is the core ROI justification for IoT variant over manual inspection

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect product variant specification (EN 179 vs. EN 1125) causing ≥1 non-conforming door installation identified at first JCI fire-safety survey
entities.rationale: JCI surveys cite door hardware non-conformance regularly; specifying wrong standard is a well-documented risk in multi-vendor hospital environments

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to document a complete hospital door inventory (type, standard, zone classification) across a 300-bed campus within 2 working days
entities.rationale: Legacy hospital buildings often lack complete door hardware documentation; survey time is a real pre-project cost

entities.full_statement: Minimize variability in wireless signal strength across reinforced-concrete hospital floors that causes >5% of installed devices to fall below reliable reporting threshold
entities.rationale: RF attenuation in reinforced concrete and metallic medical equipment environments is specific to wireless solutions; wired alternatives do not have this need

entities.full_statement: Minimize installation time per door in occupied inpatient wards to <30 minutes without requiring conduit, cables, or a licensed electrician
entities.rationale: Retrofit installation cost and clinical disruption are primary procurement objections in hospital settings

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of installation failure on non-standard door hardware configurations (glass-frame doors, narrow lever handles, legacy panic bars) in pre-1990 hospital wings
entities.rationale: Surface-mount form factor limits compatibility with non-standard door hardware; wired alarmed bars are also affected, but differently

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of wireless coverage gaps discovered post-installation requiring costly repeater additions to achieve full-campus alarm reporting coverage
entities.rationale: Post-installation coverage gaps are a known pain point for wireless security systems in complex building geometries; wired alternatives do not have this commissioning risk

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to verify single-motion door release compliance per EN 179 / EN 1125 for 100 installed devices within a 5-working-day JCI preparation window
entities.rationale: JCI surveys require documented functional testing; time pressure before unannounced surveys is a real operational constraint

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized emergency exit activation going undetected for >5 minutes during overnight or weekend reduced-staffing periods
entities.rationale: Patient elopement — especially psychiatric patients on involuntary hold — is a documented patient safety event with mortality risk. Manual patrol cannot provide real-time detection. [WEB — PSNet AHRQ elopement data; campus safety magazine]

entities.full_statement: Minimize the frequency of false alarms on high-traffic emergency exits exceeding 2 events per week due to incidental door contact by clinical staff
entities.rationale: False alarm fatigue leads to staff desensitization and ignored real events — a documented failure mode in hospital alarm management environments

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to provide authorized clinical staff with immediate key-bypass access on emergency exits during acute clinical emergencies in <10 seconds without triggering a facility-wide alarm response
entities.rationale: Hospital staff responding to resuscitation or rapid response events need unimpeded movement; cumbersome key overrides create friction in high-stakes scenarios

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to monitor battery status for all exit devices across a 200+ door hospital campus from a single dashboard with >95% real-time accuracy
entities.rationale: Manual battery inspection rounds are the only current method; battery depletion on a psychiatric ward exit device is an unacceptable safety gap

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of alarm event transmission failures exceeding 1% due to RF interference from hospital imaging equipment (MRI suites, CT rooms) in adjacent zones
entities.rationale: MRI suites generate significant electromagnetic interference; wired alternatives do not have wireless transmission failure risk

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to correlate emergency exit alarm events with concurrent patient elopement incidents in the incident reporting system with <30 seconds of timestamp resolution
entities.rationale: Root cause analysis of elopement events requires matched event timestamps; without this, incident reports remain speculative

entities.full_statement: Minimize the time required to reconfigure exit device zone assignments when hospital wards are repurposed or patient population mix changes, to <2 hours per ward
entities.rationale: Hospital ward functions change frequently (COVID surge units, seasonal capacity adjustments); reconfiguration agility is a real operational need

entities.full_statement: Minimize the effort required to replace batteries across all campus exit devices within a planned maintenance window without disrupting clinical operations in adjacent rooms
entities.rationale: Battery replacement is a maintenance burden specific to battery-operated devices; wired and RTLS alternatives require different maintenance operations

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to generate Joint Commission-compliant Environment of Care documentation for all emergency exit security devices covering 12 months of operational logs without manual data aggregation
entities.rationale: JCI Environment of Care standard EC.02.06.01 requires documented inspection and testing of egress components; manual log compilation is a significant administrative burden [WEB — Joint Commission standards]

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of GDPR-regulated door event logs containing data attributable to identifiable individuals without a legally established processing basis under EU 2016/679
entities.rationale: IoT-logged door events in EU hospitals may constitute personal data if attributable to identified employees or patients; GDPR DPIA required for systematic monitoring — constraint C8

entities.full_statement: Minimize variability in alarm trigger response time across door types (EN 179 lever-handle vs. EN 1125 panic-bar configurations) to within ±0.2 seconds under equivalent activation force
entities.rationale: Mixed door hardware types across hospital wings must deliver uniform alarm response to prevent security blind spots from unexpected timing variance

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to demonstrate real-time emergency exit compliance status to hospital leadership and external surveyors on demand, without manual inspection rounds [KPI: on-demand compliance availability rate]
entities.rationale: P3: Facilities Director becomes 'the accreditation-ready safety officer' — product must deliver visible compliance mastery automatically

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of visible emergency exit compliance gaps during an unannounced Joint Commission Environment of Care survey [KPI: non-conformance findings per survey cycle]
entities.rationale: P4: JCI surveyor, CMS inspector, and hospital board must see 'proactive, data-driven safety program' — product must generate visible audit trail at survey time

entities.full_statement: Minimize the delay between an emergency exit activation event and receipt of an actionable location-specific alert on the security officer's device to <15 seconds [KPI: alert-to-dispatch time] [hypothesis]
entities.rationale: P5 [hypothesis]: On-duty security staff dread discovering a psychiatric patient elopement hours after it occurred — real-time location-specific alerting converts helplessness into control
Von Duprin, Corbin Russwin — hardwired alarmed exit bars dominate existing hospital installations; ~40% estimated install base share
GfS Basic, ABUS — lowest-cost mechanical alarm solution; no monitoring capability; commonly used in lower-priority zones
dormakaba SafeRoute, GEZE SecuLogic — higher-cost wireless systems; typically BACnet-integrated; growing share in new hospital construction
Allegion — provides 15-second delayed egress per NFPA 101 exception; used in psychiatric wards; requires power; ~10% share estimate
Securitas Healthcare, CenTrak — adjacent L5 node; serves S1/S2 elopement use case via patient tagging; existential alternative for psychiatric/dementia segments
